### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-01-09
Hi. 

I posted about this earlier in the middle a thread, but it appears that the thread got jailed after an argument broke out or something.

Anyway, I know that many people are interested in testing out the new Wayland Display Server, but many people can't test it without building themselves, which can be a very difficult task.


I am working on a Wayland Live CD here: [https://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/](https://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/) Right now, it does have the Wayland Display server on it, but it does not yet have the nested x server, or the Wayland versions of any toolkits that are ported to Wayland. I still need to figure out how to build those...

Wayland can work in a window under X, or it can run in a TTY. It does not work in virtual box at all, due to the drivers not supporting it, and I only tested on Intel hardware. 

If you want to run it in a tty, you must run ```
sudo bash
weston
```because sudo removes the LD_LIBRARY_PATH variable, which is needed.

believe it or not, Wayland apparently already supports spanning across two monitors. I attached a picture of it running. The garbage and ghosting on the first screen is due to a faulty screen, which is going to be replaced.


As for the name of the project, yes. I AM a fan of her.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-01-10
> **cecilpierce said:**
> @nerdopolis

How do I get wayland or weston to run ?

I get failed nouveau and libEGL errors, I did lsmod | grep nouveau and it says its loaded.
I didnt install it, just running the live dvd, it looks real nice though  :)

Thanks
Are you running 'sudo weston'? you need to run 'sudo bash' then just run weston, because sudo drops the LD_LIBRARY_PATH variable, which is needed for it to find the custom libraries. 

It only needs to be root if you are running it in a tty though. If you want to run it in a window in X, there should be an icon in the desktop folder for that.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-01-18
Hi.
In this build, every toolkit built, but I didn't test it fully. It does seem that the QT version of Wayland can find the plugin that allows it to run Wayland. 

The problem is the login is broken in this one for the Desktop. The fix for that is fixed in the build script, which takes HOURS to run, but on this one, to get it to login to the desktop, you need to do on the live cd:
switch to a tty and run:
```
sudo apt-get remove gnome-session
sudo service kdm restart
```and the default desktop should come up.



To run the Wayland apps, start Wayland, either by running it on the Desktop, or switching to a tty, and running
```
sudo westoncalller
```, which is now a script that handles the necessary environment variables. 



To run the Wayland apps, you need to run them in a Wayland terminal, because it will have the environment variables needed, which you can open in Wayland. 


QT programs are in /usr/local/examples. (they are QT5). Most of the files in /usr/local/bin are the test clutter apps, while to to test a GTK app in Wayland, just run a standard gnome app in the Wayland terminal.

Download is here: [EDIT: it appears it's not on any mirrors!?]
                                    [https://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/EverythingBuildsDoesntStart/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso/download]("http://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/EverythingBuildsDoesntStart/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso/download")
 . I admit I did not yet test it on my box, because it does not work in Virtualbox, and I need to shutdown to test it.

EDIT: I also forgot, to run a QT app in Wayland, (the ones in /usr/local/examples) you need to specify the extra option ```
-platform wayland
``` to the command. I forgot to set the environment variable in westoncaller. The next ISO should have that, and working autologin AFTER a startup prompt for choosing between X or Wayland.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-01-26
OK. I have the new livecd [https://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/latest/download](https://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/latest/download) . This one has Wayland. QT, GTK, and Clutter toolkits that have Wayland ports. It also has the rootless X display server for Wayland. I'll try to get a good video of Wayland... 

To run Wayland in a tty, you need to run ```
sudo westoncaller
``` . 

To move windows, click on them while holding the super (windows) key, as decorations are client side, and the toolkits don't have them yet.

The QT apps and the Clutter apps are located in /usr/local/bin . KDE apps I think need to be recompiled to use the new version of QT.

GTK apps can be run from a Wayland terminal. Not all of them run, but gedit does work. gedit also has a resize handle, so you can try out the performance of resizes. 

I don't have the EFL toolkit on it yet. 

You can launch the rootless x server by clicking the X.org logo on the Wayland panel. It creates DISPLAY :2, but the windows don't refresh, and draw weird.


EDIT: I now have a video here: _[http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F9x8gXno-P0](http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F9x8gXno-P0)_[]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=F9x8gXno-P0&feature=youtu.be")

Sorry about the needing to start the desktop manually. That'll be fixed, and that's because of my Grub mistake.

glxgears is running in x on that screenshot. I think you just want 'gears' that's the Wayland version of it. The next CD will startup to Wayland by default.

The Wayland programs, other then GTK apps are in /usr/local/bin (not /usr/bin) If it's in /usr/local/bin, it should work in wayland, otherwise it'll start in X, unless it's a GTK app, then it Might work in Wayland.

The panel on the top right now is limited, it doesn't allow text for describing what the icons would do. I'll try to add more though.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-02-08
Hi. I finally have an updated ISO with EFL now, and multiple fixes, and a newer version of Wayland with the input redirection patches, as those patches where applied after the old ISO was made. 

Wayland even works to some degree on my box on VirtualBox, nested under X. I still have some glitches, but I don't know if thats because of the fact I don't have a CPU with virtualization that makes it flaky or not

[http://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/RebeccaBlackLinux%2002-08-2012.iso/download]("http://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/RebeccaBlackLinux%2002-08-2012.iso/download")

EDIT: It allows you to try to start directly to Wayland, running as *ROOT*, but it ends up hanging. I JUST found out why, and it's because I'm missing the HOME variable, once it starts from init. I'll have a new ISO with that fix... soon...

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-02-11
Ok. I have the new live image out, which allows you to start directly to a Wayland server by default, if it's supported (otherwise it falls back to X).

When you install it, which I still don't recommend, it only currently boots to Wayland, and you can't choose X.

If you switch out KDM with another display manager, it will go to X, because I modified the KDM startup script.

Wayland also starts as ROOT when started directly, so be careful about what commands you run, or leaving your computer unattended.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-02-12
Ok a simple script .... just to run 3 things .... as a start to make sure things work

```

#! /bin/bash
# script to run clock gradients then puzzle one after the other
cd /usr/local/bin
./puzzle
./gradients
./clock

```copy the above text into gedit

[COLOR=Blue]**save as s1.sh**
[/COLOR]
**chmod  u+x**  **s1.sh**

To run it ... ensure that you are in the right directory .... where s1.sh is located

./s1.sh


after you close one program the next one will run ..... simple as ..... works in X ok
but the close option is not there in Wayland ..... so how do we add a border to a
frame - and have the close button ...... or to right click on the running window
to get to close it .....


This works in the Wayland window ..... from E17 ...... if you want to try it - but this will go
after you remove the DVD ......
sudo apt-get update
**sudo apt-get install e17**

then run the command below in the wayland terminal the window stays inside
of the wayland desktop .... so I am presuming it is using wayland .... will post a video shortly

elementary_test [**[COLOR=Red]VIDEO in WAYLAND[/COLOR]**]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=_CYC3j1jyRY&feature=youtu.be")

I have windows7 and Ubuntu on this ....

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-20
I'd like to try to clear up some confusion I'm seeing in this thread.

The program you seem to be calling wayland is not wayland.  It's a program called weston.  The one with the terminal with the yellow border, and the panel at the top with a few icons.  While for X, there is actually a single program called "X", there is no program called wayland.  Wayland is a *protocol*, not a program.  A protocol for allowing applications, including a window manager, to talk directly to your video hardware.  So all existing window managers are expected to add support for the wayland protocol, by using the wayland libraries, so they can become wayland compositors.  So the complaints about the interface that weston provides are pointless, you'll get the full window managers you've known and loved in time.

Weston is just an example compositor to play with until full fledged compositors are ported / developed.

> **23dornot23d said:**
> already this is the only Distro that picks up my TV and uses it properly ....

This has NOTHING to do with wayland or any work nerdopolis has done.  Probably 99.99% of this CD is just the next release of kubuntu, which is ubuntu + the KDE desktop.  Anything you do with this CD outside of weston you could do just as easily, if not easier, if you just downloaded the latest kubuntu Precise 12.04 image from ubuntu.

I saw a couple questions of why more people aren't coming to this thread to talk about the possible amazing future of wayland.  Why would they do that, when it has already been done on the wayland mailing list?

Yes, you can do fully 3D, Minority Report style desktops with wayland.  Just like you could have done with compiz and other compositing window managers.  With wayland you just don't have X in the way anymore.  3D desktops have been around for a long time.  The problem is coming up with something practical enough that anybody will use it.

With wayland, your application allocates a buffer (image) directly in your video card's memory, draws to it using OpenGL (can easily be hardware accelerated 3D), then tells the compositor (window manager) it's done drawing a frame, and the compositor merges that application's output with the others to create the full desktop image (compositing), writes that composite back to video memory, and tells the video card to output it to your display.  

"You could just as easily make crazy things like, wrap each window around a 3d ball, and have all your windows bouncing around your screen. When you click on a ball, it opens up flat on your screen for you to work with. Any of the slick Minority Report-like UI&#8217;s in the movies would be relatively easy to make. The kicker is all of these environments would be equally easy to code." - [http://kdubois.net/?p=1217](http://kdubois.net/?p=1217)


I'm very impressed with what nerdopolis has achieved with this live cd, and it's great to see people discovering what does and doesn't work.

---

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-20
> **nerdopolis said:**
> Maybe I can instead figure out why some GNOME apps fail with GTKwayland to GTKx11 cast errors...

I get the impression a lot of gtk apps actually use a combination of gtk plus some X library stuff.  That won't work.  Porting to wayland requires removing the X library calls - pure gtk.  I'm not familiar with the errors you're seeing, but my guess is this is what you're hitting.

Will now try to understand what [[COLOR=Blue]_**Wayland does ..... Protocol**_[/COLOR]]("http://arstechnica.com/open-source/guides/2011/03/the-linux-graphics-stack-from-x-to-wayland.ars")

> 
Quote from it ...... 

[B]The new API was to be called GEM (Graphics Execution Manager) - 
Dave Airlie had developed a memory management module they called  translation table maps (TTM). 
TTM was designed to move the memory  buffers destined for graphics devices back and forth between graphic  device memory and system memory.[/B]

Both TTM and GEM try to solve the 3D acceleration problem in the Linux  graphics stack by integrating directly with X to get to the device to  perform GPU operations. Both attempt to bring order to the crowd of  libraries like OpenGL (which depends on X), Qt (depends on X) and GTK+  (also X). The problem was that X stands between all of these libraries  and the kernel, and the kernel is the way to the device driver, and the  ultimately to the GPU.
the article tends to explain X .... but will look for more information on it .....

Passing data to a stack and then passing it to the graphics frame buffer .... how it does this is maybe something I should be looking at to fully understand what is going on here.

 yaourt -S wayland
```

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-27
> **23dornot23d said:**
> * ( would like a link to some laymans diagrams showing in detail the workings of this - a flowchart maybe of where it is now )

Diagram is at the top of the page on the first link from the main wayland page:  [http://wayland.freedesktop.org/architecture.html](http://wayland.freedesktop.org/architecture.html)

It might help to think, when you look at the second diagram, replace "wayland compositor" with "window manager".  

> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
all good documentation to show how weston and wayland work ..... 
Well, start by reading all of [http://wayland.freedesktop.org/](http://wayland.freedesktop.org/) if you haven't.  I recently came across some additional potentially interesting documentation here:  [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/attachments/20111023/49457c80/attachment-0003.pdf](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/attachments/20111023/49457c80/attachment-0003.pdf)
Specifically the Wayland Compositor.pdf attachment.


> **cbowman57 said:**
> You're gonna have to sell me on Wayland.  What good is it?  What does it do? etc...

Eh, you're welcome to just wait for it to become your default desktop like most people.  

Basically, X is a LOT of crufty old code that mostly doesn't get used anymore and nobody wants to maintain, and a tiny bit of new shiny stuff.  A lot of the new shiny stuff isn't even in X anymore, it's moved out to the kernel, and other libraries.  So wayland is just (X developers) scrapping the cruft, taking all the shiny stuff, and tying it together with a minimal protocol.  That protocol is called wayland.

There is no one wayland server, as there is (basically) one X sever.  All existing (compositing) window managers are expected to directly implement the wayland protocol themselves using the wayland libraries, communicate with the input hardware with other existing libraries (evdev), and *become* the display server.

---

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-27
> **23dornot23d said:**
> Launchpad bug - add to this ...... [https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gtk+3.0/+bug/954352](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/gtk+3.0/+bug/954352)
[[COLOR=Blue][/COLOR]]("http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-March/002585.html")
Guess once Precise is out of the way the real development can begin ......


I think that bug is actually a huge problem.  I really don't know how Ubuntu will ever enable GTK+ support for wayland, which is critical for making it usable.  All because Nvidia chooses to build their proprietary driver in a way that doesn't work with shared memory.  There are a couple options, but I don't see anything budging.  Very importantly, there's a problem preventing solving this problem by just providing one set of packages for people using the Nvidia proprietary driver, and another set for those who want to use GTK+ with wayland.  It's all in that bug.

If it's not clear, the GTK+ support for wayland is critical because that's exactly how all existing gnome related applications are expected to communicate with a wayland display server.  The only other ways for a client to communicate via wayland are Qt, Clutter, EFL, possibly SDL, and directly implementing the wayland protocol (which is expected to be rare).

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-27
Video I did several days ago of the webkit browser running in weston (wayland):
[http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/demos/2012-03-22-weston-qtwebkit-youtube.ogv](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/demos/2012-03-22-weston-qtwebkit-youtube.ogv)

The video is clear and smooth.  If it doesn't look that way to you, you have a playback problem.

WebKit, as you may know, is the core of Chrome and Safari.  

So yeah, there's plenty left to be done, but I think this is pretty cool.
And I have a build script to download, build, install, and run all of wayland/weston v0.85 with GTK+ and Qt with qtwebkit:  [http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/)

Takes a total of about 3 hours 16 minutes for me to get through all that.

Definitely worth joining the #wayland channel on irc.freenode.net if you're trying to build wayland stuff.

### Post by cbowman57 on 2012-03-27
Keith, I'm actually more convinced that the problem I ran into was dependencies left in place by the proprietary Nvidia driver.  My connection is still slow too so I became frustrated & didn't want to keep repeating the cycle.

The couple hours I spent did convince me that building & installing from the AUR is the way to go. I might give it another go the next time I have a completely fresh Arch installation before I start adding drivers & things to it.

I was initially confused too because I thought I was building wayland, which went without a hitch but obviously isn't everything I needed.  By using the weston or weston-git from AUR it pulled in everything, including wayland.

### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-28
> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
it got so far and stopped - cured that ..... with .....
[B]
aptitude install llvm                                              [/B]
I'm very curious why.  That script is supposed to install llvm.

> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
almost got there ..... this time .....
> Cloning into 'qtjsondb'...
fatal: The remote end hung up unexpectedly

Yeah, that's a qt5 download failure.  My nightmare all last weekend.  You should probably try downloading qt5 before running that script, until the download succeeds, then the script will notice the downloaded source and use it:

mkdir ~/source; cd ~/source
git clone git://gitorious.org/qt/qt5 # 0:38.56elapsed
perl init-repository # 1:00:19elapsed - this is the problem
~/wayland-build-ubuntu-0.85.sh # 2:15:13elapsed

#qt-labs on irc.freenode.net is the place to talk about qt5 problems.  And maybe complain about the lack of handling download failures in their init-repositories script.

Or you could disable building qt5 and qtwebkit at by setting $BUILD_QT at the top of the script to 0.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-03-30
The latest version and download that Cecil mentioned has the later installer on it

I am up and running now on my Test Computer on a USB HDD ..... this is good ;)

[IMG]http://img854.imageshack.us/img854/2650/screenshotat20120330081.jpg[/IMG]

ty Cecil .... plus setting it as a primary partition is a must too .....

The graphics - wireless and Gnome-Shell are all working great .....

Text is the only downside - its not as clear as ubuntu

Now to see if pacman works .... and then back to Wayland ..... we can work both systems now .....

dont believe it ..... same scenario ....

>

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-03-30
This link here ..... is on the distrowatch only ..... at the bottom of the text .....

[IMG]http://img825.imageshack.us/img825/4675/screenshotat20120330145.jpg[/IMG]

Here is the DVD download below saves you searching ...... 2.8 Gig ....

[http://sourceforge.net/projects/kahelos/files/KahelOS-LiveDVDdesktop-020212-i686.iso/download](http://sourceforge.net/projects/kahelos/files/KahelOS-LiveDVDdesktop-020212-i686.iso/download)

its still not uptodate .... but its the latest release ....... with the install to partition on it

good to have a Arch I can get on my USBs though .... nice ......

will sort out the problems no doubt ..... but why didn't they do it so everyone can
use it without a load more hassle ... not complaining though ... as getting it to install
to the USB HD and also pick up my TV is a definate .... big well done to the creators of it ....

My history ..... had to remove all the extensions and some unused display drivers .... 

Before it would upgrade ..... with pacman -Syu

then it throws back a lot of gpg errors - which are cured ..... at the end ..... shown below .....

is all my history ..... of sorting the dependancy problem out on my machine ..... yours
may differ .... but you will see what it complains about ...... then you will know what
to remove ....... ( should always keep a record - as if there is something you need - it often can be added back in
at the end of the process )

> 
   90  sudo pacman -Syu
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-apps-menu
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-auto-move-windows
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-dock
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-drive-menu
sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-gajim
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-alternate-tab
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-alternative-status-menu
   sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-native-window-placement

  sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-windows-navigator

  sudo pacman -R gnome-shell-extension-xrandr-indicator
  sudo pacman -R mach64-dri
  sudo pacman -R xf86-video-mach64
  sudo pacman -R mach64-dri
  sudo pacman -R mga-dri
  sudo pacman -R xf86-video-mga
  sudo pacman -R mga-dri
  sudo pacman -R r128-dri
sudo pacman -R xf86-video-r128
  sudo pacman -R r128-dri
  sudo pacman -R savage-dri
  sudo pacman -R xf86-video-savage
  sudo pacman -R savage-dri
  sudo pacman -R sis-dri
  sudo pacman -R xf86-video-sis
  sudo pacman -R sis-dri
  sudo pacman -R tdfx-dri
  sudo pacman -R xf86-video-tdfx
  sudo pacman -R tdfx-dri
  sudo pacman -Syu
[COLOR=Red]**sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf /etc/pacman.conf.old**[/COLOR]
  sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf.pacnew /etc/pacman.conf
  sudo pacman-key --init

 [COLOR=Red][B]sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf /etc/pacman.conf.old

I actually copied this the pacman.conf ...... to keep a backup of the original .....
as you may want to compare them later on.[/B][/COLOR]

> 
sudo pacman -Syu
sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf.pacnew /etc/pacman.conf
sudo pacman-key --init
sudo pacman -Syu

Comment by: rjack on Sat, 12 Nov 2011 20:41:27 +0000
How can I install this in a x64 system?

[gcm@minimo adobe-air-sdk]$ makepkg -s
== Making package: adobe-air-sdk 2.6-1 (Sat Nov 12 21:36:43 CET 2011)

Comment by: rjack on Tue, 15 Nov 2011 00:31:23 +0000
Solved, I just had to enable the multilib repository: [https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Arch64_FAQ#Can_I_run_32-bit_apps_inside_Arch64.3F](https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/Arch64_FAQ#Can_I_run_32-bit_apps_inside_Arch64.3F)    

Comment by: prurigro on Wed, 16 Nov 2011 04:32:43 +0000
@rjack: would you recommend any tweaks to the PKGBUILD based on what you needed to do?    

Comment by: rjack on Wed, 30 Nov 2011 14:42:33 +0000
@prurigro maybe a comment at the beginning of the file telling that the user must have the [multilib] repository enabled in /etc/pacman.conf    

Comment by: felixonmars on Thu, 08 Mar 2012 07:12:19 +0000
Please add dependence: lib32-gtk2    

Comment by: prurigro on Thu, 08 Mar 2012 09:57:11 +0000
felixonmars: done :)    

First Submitted: Fri, 17 Apr 2009 11:06:05 +0000    
adobe-air-sdk 2.6-2 
( Unsupported package: Potentially dangerous ! )
==> Edit PKGBUILD ? [Y/n] ("A" to abort)
==> ------------------------------------
==> y

Please add $EDITOR to your environment variables
for example:
export EDITOR="vim" (in ~/.bashrc)
(replace vim with your favorite editor)

==> Edit PKGBUILD with:  

used gedit

> 
# Contributor: Prurigro
# Maintainer: Prurigro

pkgname=adobe-air-sdk
pkgver=2.6
pkgrel=2
pkgdesc="Adobe Air Software Development Kit and Runtime Environment"
arch=('i686' 'x86_64')
url="http://get.adobe.com/air/"
license=('custom')
if [[ $CARCH == x86_64 ]]; then
        depends=('lib32-gcc-libs' 'lib32-libxml2' 'lib32-nss' 'lib32-gtk2')
fi
if [[ $CARCH == i686 ]]; then
        depends=('gcc-libs' 'libxml2' 'nss' 'gtk2')
fi
source=('http://airdownload.adobe.com/air/lin/download/latest/AdobeAIRSDK.tbz2')
md5sums=('5466178bf7764f043d2225e6d018319c')
install=${pkgname}.install

build (){
    install -d ${pkgdir}/opt/adobe-air-sdk || return 1
    pushd ${srcdir} || return 1
        find . ! -name AdobeAIRSDK.tbz2 ! -name AIR\ SDK\ license.pdf | sed -e 's/\.\///g' | while read file; do
            if [ -d "$file" ]; then
                install -d ${pkgdir}/opt/adobe-air-sdk/"$file" || return 1
            elif [ -h "$file" ]; then
                if [ $(file "$file" | grep 'broken' -c) = "0" ]; then

Qt5 appears to be in neither Keith, you'll have to build & install it from what I'm reading.

Would that be a component of KDE?

Looks good otherwise though, and the nice thing about building with arch, most of the tools are already in it or imported as dependencies with little interaction. ### Post by Darxus on 2012-03-30
> **23dornot23d said:**
> Will try now ....... was using the instructions on the Wayland page

did not realise the script would be ok for Arch ..... 

I will try it now .....

"If run on Ubuntu Oneric, it will also install the rest of the dependencies as Ubuntu packages.  If run on another distro, you can run it repeatedly to find out what dependency you need to install next (set CLEAN to nothing)." - [http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/)

If you run it on ubuntu, it installs some packages to resolve some of the dependencies.  If you run it on anything else, it skips package installation, and you'll have to figure out for yourself what else you need to install by reading the build errors (as I did for ubuntu).

If you get an error like "No package 'gdk-pixbuf-2.0' found" you need to figure out which distro package contains the file gdk-pixbuf-2.0.pc .  So on ubuntu I'd do "apt-file search /gdk-pixbuf-2.0.pc".

All 40 of the packages installed by that script fixed a different build error.  

I haven't really been paying attention to how you're building stuff on arch.  There's probably a better way than using my script.  But building qt5 would be one of the last steps, not the first.  

I don't know if this has been mentioned in this thread, but this is some info on building wayland on arch:  [http://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?ID=30350](http://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?ID=30350)

You should probably join #wayland on irc.freenode.net for help.

So first sort some dependancies out

> 
Requires libxcb >= 1.5.

Required packages:
libxcb1 libxcb1-dev libx11-xcb1 libx11-xcb-dev libxcb-keysyms1 libxcb-keysyms1-dev libxcb-image0 libxcb-image0-dev libxcb-shm0 libxcb-shm0-dev libxcb-icccm1 libxcb-icccm1-dev libxcb-sync0 libxcb-sync0-dev libxcb-render-util0 libxcb-render-util0-dev libxcb-xfixes0-dev libxrender-dev libxcb-shape0-dev

On Ubuntu 11.10 icccm1 is replaced by icccm4 and xcb-render-util is not available:
libxcb1 libxcb1-dev libx11-xcb1 libx11-xcb-dev libxcb-keysyms1 libxcb-keysyms1-dev libxcb-image0 libxcb-image0-dev libxcb-shm0 libxcb-shm0-dev libxcb-icccm4 libxcb-icccm4-dev libxcb-sync0 libxcb-sync0-dev libxcb-xfixes0-dev libxrender-dev libxcb-shape0-dev
The packages for xcb-render-util can be installed manually from [http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/libxcb-render-util0](http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/libxcb-render-util0) and [http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/libxcb-render-util0-dev](http://packages.ubuntu.com/natty/libxcb-render-util0-dev)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2012-03-31
This is one of the first things I do when I set up a fresh install of Arch, in the /etc/makepkg.conf you can change the CFLAGS & CXXFLAGS lines to this: ```
CFLAGS="-march=native -O2 -pipe -fstack-protector --param=ssp-buffer-size=4 -D_FORTIFY_SOURCE=2"
CXXFLAGS="${CFLAGS}"
``` that will make it compile for the CPU architecture, which might also make it a little faster.

This is a crazy statement .... and question ...

[COLOR=Red]**wayland and wayland-git are in conflict. Remove wayland-git? [y/N] n**[/COLOR]

you cannot remove wayland separately ..... and why would you ....
and to remove wayland-git removes everything weston just loaded up ...... BIZARRE

as even the Arch wiki says what is required

> 
**  Requirements **

 Currently Wayland will only work with on a system that is utilizing [KMS]("https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/KMS"). 
 **  Installation **

 You need to build and install [FONT=monospace][libxkbcommon-git]("https://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?K=libxkbcommon-git&SeB=x")[/FONT], [FONT=monospace][wayland-git]("https://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?K=wayland-git&SeB=x")[/FONT] (or [FONT=monospace][libxkbcommon]("https://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?K=libxkbcommon&SeB=x")[/FONT], [FONT=monospace][wayland]("https://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?K=wayland&SeB=x")[/FONT]) and [FONT=monospace][mesa-full-wayland]("https://aur.archlinux.org/packages.php?K=mesa-full-wayland&SeB=x")[/FONT] from [AUR]("https://wiki.archlinux.org/index.php/AUR"). Replacement of the Mesa stack is required because the stock one does not support Wayland.

Please add libegl dependency to PKGBUILD ;)    

Comment by: KriK on Wed, 24 Aug 2011 11:39:38 +0000
For wayland is also needed --enable-glesv2=yes    

Comment by: alip on Wed, 24 Aug 2011 13:58:54 +0000
updated. according to [http://wayland.freedesktop.org/building.html](http://wayland.freedesktop.org/building.html) it is not needed though. what did you experience?    

Comment by: chenxiaolong on Sat, 27 Aug 2011 19:48:43 +0000
glesv2 and gl cannot be enabled at the same time. Please take a look at: [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/cairo/2011-July/022138.html](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/cairo/2011-July/022138.html)

You could add a comment about this in the PKGBUILD.    

Comment by: alip on Sat, 27 Aug 2011 20:58:21 +0000
then removed again. I didn't made research on the compilation failure, just thought it was some temporary error crept into the code. Thanks for the clarification.    

Comment by: Samsagax on Sun, 18 Sep 2011 23:02:53 +0000
The provides array is quoted wrong:
provides=('cairo=${_realver}' 'cairo-git' 'cairo-xcb=${_realver}')

Should use soft-quoting otherwise the ${_realver} argument won't be parsed correctly:
provides=(cairo=${_realver} 'cairo-git' cairo-xcb=${_realver})    

First Submitted: Tue, 02 Nov 2010 11:34:05 +0000

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-04-01
Check this post out

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11807624&postcount=279](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11807624&postcount=279)

keep a copy of the old one too

**[COLOR=Blue]sudo cp /etc/pacman.conf[B] /etc/pacmanold.conf**
[/COLOR][/B]
Then these two commands .....

 you need to do


[COLOR=Blue]**sudo mv /etc/pacman.conf.pacnew /etc/pacman.conf**[/COLOR]
 [COLOR=Blue]**sudo pacman-key --init**[/COLOR]

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-01
> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/tmp
  export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR


Yup.

> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
maybe that is all that is in the 

/home/keith/.config/weston.ini


You don't need that.  It will just read it if it's there.  The differences in appearance were because creating that .ini file caused it to fail to find the background and panel/launcher textures, because the defaults worked and the .ini didn't.

# If upgrades are available for these packages they will be asked for first
SyncFirst   = pacman
#XferCommand = /usr/bin/curl -C - -f %u > %o
#XferCommand = /usr/bin/wget --passive-ftp -c -O %o %u
#CleanMethod = KeepInstalled
Architecture = auto

# Pacman won't upgrade packages listed in IgnorePkg and members of IgnoreGroup
#IgnorePkg   =
#IgnoreGroup =

#NoUpgrade   =
#NoExtract   =

# Misc options
#UseSyslog
#UseDelta
#TotalDownload
CheckSpace
#VerbosePkgLists

# PGP signature checking
[COLOR=Red]**# NOTE: None of this will work without running `pacman-key --init` first.**[/COLOR]
# The compiled in default is equivalent to the following line. This requires
# you to locally sign and trust packager keys using `pacman-key` for them to be
# considered valid.
#SigLevel = Optional TrustedOnly
# If you wish to check signatures but avoid local sign and trust issues, use
# the following line. This will treat any key imported into pacman's keyring as
# trusted.
#SigLevel = Optional TrustAll
# For now, off by default unless you read the above.
SigLevel = Never

#
# REPOSITORIES
#   - can be defined here or included from another file
#   - pacman will search repositories in the order defined here
#   - local/custom mirrors can be added here or in separate files
#   - repositories listed first will take precedence when packages
#     have identical names, regardless of version number
#   - URLs will have $repo replaced by the name of the current repo
#   - URLs will have $arch replaced by the name of the architecture
#
# Repository entries are of the format:
#       [repo-name]
#       Server = ServerName
#       Include = IncludePath
#
# The header [repo-name] is crucial - it must be present and
# uncommented to enable the repo.
#

# The testing repositories are disabled by default. To enable, uncomment the
# repo name header and Include lines. You can add preferred servers immediately
# after the header, and they will be used before the default mirrors.

#[testing]
#SigLevel = PackageRequired
#Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

[core]
#SigLevel = PackageRequired
Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

[extra]
#SigLevel = PackageOptional
Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

#[community-testing]
#SigLevel = PackageRequired
#Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

[community]
#SigLevel = PackageOptional
Include = /etc/pacman.d/mirrorlist

# An example of a custom package repository.  See the pacman manpage for
# tips on creating your own repositories.
#[custom]
[archlinuxfr] 
[COLOR=Red]**Server = http://repo.archlinux.fr/$arch**[/COLOR]

#SigLevel = Optional TrustAll
#Server = file:///home/custompkgs

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-04-01
Ok half of what I had for memory (4 Gig on mine ) ..... but it still should be ok ...... as long as nothing else is
running as you are doing it .......

it will come up like this at the end ..... then follow the instructions below this

> 
loading packages...
resolving dependencies...
looking for inter-conflicts...
:: mesa-full-wayland and mesa are in conflict. Remove mesa? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and libgl are in conflict. Remove libgl? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and libglapi are in conflict. Remove libglapi? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and libgles are in conflict. Remove libgles? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and libegl are in conflict. Remove libegl? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and ati-dri are in conflict. Remove ati-dri? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and intel-dri are in conflict. Remove intel-dri? [y/N] y
:: mesa-full-wayland and nouveau-dri are in conflict. Remove nouveau-dri? [y/N] y
also you need to remove

**sudo yaourt -R xf86-video-vmware**
and
[COLOR=Blue][COLOR=Black]**yaourt -R svga-dri**

before the next build ......

and move one file ...... its for android devices from what is here [[COLOR=Blue]_**link**_[/COLOR]]("https://www.google.com/search?q=arch+khrplatform.h&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8&aq=t&rls=org.mozilla:en-US:official&client=firefox-a")
( as this gets recreated - and will stop the install if you do not do it )

[/COLOR][/COLOR][B]sudo mv khrplatform.h khrplatformbackup.h


Then
 [COLOR=Blue]
[COLOR=Black]yaourt -Syy weston
[/COLOR][/COLOR][COLOR=Blue][COLOR=Black]yaourt -Syy [/COLOR]weston-git[/COLOR][/B]

then as root type in weston ( and if all is well you will have a weston window pop up

#[COLOR=Red][B]weston


[[COLOR=Blue]_NEW VIDEO_[/COLOR]]("http://www.youtube.com/watch?v=peJteIY5eqE&feature=youtu.be")[/B][/COLOR]

> 
**Weston and demo applications**

  Weston is the reference implementation of a Wayland compositor. It's available in the weston repo and comes with a few demo applications.  
Aside from mesa and libxkbcommon, the Weston dependencies can be satisfied with released versions of:  gdk-pixbuf-2.0, libudev 136, libdrm 2.4.23, pixman-1, cairo-gl 1.11.3, glib-2.0, and gobject-2.0.   And optionally, for the pdf viewer: poppler-glib and gio-2.0.

$ git clone git://anongit.freedesktop.org/wayland/weston     
$ cd weston 
    $ ./autogen.sh --prefix=$WLD     
$ make 
$ make install

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-06
I don't understand why you guys to go through all this effort without reading the wayland mailing list or being in the #wayland channel on irc.freenode.net.  And #qt-labs on irc.freenode.net specifically for building qt5.

> **23dornot23d said:**
> I am seeing some build problems ..... so have now started to look for
a QT web browser ....

Found this one ..... [https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=128681](https://bbs.archlinux.org/viewtopic.php?id=128681)

The full qt5 source includes qtwebkit, which is the browser running in my wayland video:  [http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/demos/2012-03-22-weston-qtwebkit-youtube.html](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/demos/2012-03-22-weston-qtwebkit-youtube.html)

I described all of that on the wayland mailing list:  [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-March/002655.html](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-March/002655.html)


> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
[COLOR=Red]**UBUNTU - Wayland - build-0.85.sh - Fails here**[/COLOR]
  
I'd love to help you with that.  
"make[1]: Entering directory `/home/keith/source/mesa/src'"
It's mesa that's failing to build.  That error message sucks.  

mesa can get to be a bit of a mess between compiles, and seems to require cleaning more than anything else, basically "cd /home/keith/source/mesa && git clean -xfd", then re-run the script.  In the script, there's a line that says "CLEAN=", you can comment that out, and uncomment the line above it, and it'll automatically do this cleaning for you each time.  That just results in building taking longer because it has to start all over.

"git clean -xfd" should get you back to the point equivalent to deleting the mesa directory and re-running the script to have it completely re-downloaded.  

It's probably worth doing "rm -rf ~/install" at the same time, to make sure there isn't anything broken installed there that it's somehow trying to use.

If you want to try something more drastic, you can also do "rm -rf ~/source", so it has to re-download all of the source.  Although that *should* never be necessary.  You *should* get the same effect from "cd ~/source; for dir in *; do cd $dir; git clean -xfd; cd ..; done" (just git cleaning all the git repos).

If cleaning and deleting ~/install doesn't help, I'd say either there's a bug in mesa, or it's lacking a more appropriate error message (which still comes down to a bug in mesa).  I'd try asking the wayland irc channel or mailing list.  You could also try the mesa mailing list.  

A huge problem with this is most of the time people have a compile error like this, it's their own fault, they forgot something dumb.  So people who could help you often won't pay attention, assuming it's your fault and not having the time to deal with that.  


> **23dornot23d said:**
> I never use IRC as I am not that technically minded to be able to discuss the things
happening here and understand what is going on ...... when people text back.

Yeah but that's the place to ask questions of people who have a far better understanding of these things than nerdopolis or I.  Hint:  He and I are not the experts.

What is there to understand?  Download the script and run it.  That's all.  It'll ask you for your sudo password so it can install some packages.

```
wget http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/wayland-build-0.85.sh; chmod +x wayland-build-0.85.sh; ./wayland-build-0.85.sh
```

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-06
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Thanks Darxus, should I try it on a fresh install or will it work on precise that failed before ?

Thanks for the help, 
Cec (wd4lko on the irc)

Sorry I haven't managed to keep track of all the details in this 36 page thread :)

No need to do a fresh install, unless you've installed stuff in /usr/local or /.  If you've run this script before, go ahead and delete ~/install and ~/source first.


> **23dornot23d said:**
> QT dropped off again as I was downloading ....

Quoting myself from the wayland mailing list:

The qt5 download has been unreliable for me, so I run it first:

mkdir ~/source; cd ~/source
git clone git://gitorious.org/qt/qt5 # 0:38.56elapsed
perl init-repository # 1:00:19elapsed - this is the problem
~/wayland-build-ubuntu-0.85.sh # 2:15:13elapsed

- [http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-March/002765.html](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-March/002765.html)

I think I know what is wrong ....... ending up wiith 2 versions of Glibc

This is the version on a RB2 fresh install ....... but when we pull the glibc-dev in we end up with
2 versions ..... 2.32 and 2.31.17 * found this out when I installed GIMP 2.8 as it would not let
me build that ......

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11818229&postcount=3](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11818229&postcount=3)

> Then we stopped ..... is there an easy way to point this to the correct one
I have come across this before and not known how to resolve the double
version issue .....

Not going as planned as all the dependencies need loading in - when we start with a RB version

Then they are all there for us ...... ( also other things are set up too )

________________________________________________________

> Well I did a fresh kubuntu 12.04 install and tried the script again and got this, pic.
:confused:
                                                                                                              Attached Thumbnails                                           [[IMG]http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=215717&stc=1&thumb=1&d=1333905165[/IMG]]("http://ubuntuforums.org/attachment.php?attachmentid=215717&d=1333905165")                        
                                                                                                    
That is the initial error ..... caused by a missing library .... Darxus got us by this .....

[http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11813355&postcount=331](http://ubuntuforums.org/showpost.php?p=11813355&postcount=331)

Make sure that the system is uptodate ....

sudo apt-get update

sudo apt-get install aptitude

sudo aptitude update

sudo aptitude safe-upgrade

sudo aptitude install synaptic

sudo aptitude install libmapi.dev


( think this solved that and then - clean out the source and install directory then

 re-run the 0.85 script )



________________________________________________________

Am just about to go through it again - the build fails on QT5 again ..... not sure why

maybe the 7 Gig or so that it all takes up ...... with wayland and everything else .... mesa etc ...

I have run this a number of times - but the build worked a lot better on my other machine

8 cores - was pretty fast at compiling things - but I left it going while I did something else ...

Still its got the odd error - but it ran ok on the Kubuntu clean build ......

The other RBv3 when I tried - after 3 hours or so - needed to build gtk+ or something

and that was when I got the glibc problems .... and I know from the past - this one 

stops me in my tracks - search on [[COLOR=Blue]_**glibc 2 versions**_[/COLOR]]("http://www.google.com/search?client=ubuntu&channel=fs&q=glibc+2+versions&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8#hl=en&client=ubuntu&hs=pwI&channel=fs&sclient=psy-ab&q=solved+glibc+2+versions&oq=solved+glibc+2+versions&aq=f&aqi=&aql=&gs_l=serp.3...36913l40240l0l40536l13l10l3l0l0l1l187l1405l0j10l16l0.frgbld.&pbx=1&bav=on.2,or.r_gc.r_pw.r_qf.,cf.osb&fp=3bccbf4365edf3c2&biw=1048&bih=523") ..... not found one answer that

Got another night to see where it leads now .......

But the stopping point at the moment is building QT5

My install/bin area now looks like this ....... ( so - seems like most things are there ) ...... qmake was my
last error for QT5 to work .... and to compile applications ......

[IMG]http://img269.imageshack.us/img269/7361/snapshot7h.jpg[/IMG]


Am going to re-build QT5 from here again .....

> 
QT5 Alpha download 285 Meg Source ....

[http://qt-project.org/wiki/Qt-5-Alpha](http://qt-project.org/wiki/Qt-5-Alpha)

Download [http://releases.qt-project.org/qt5.0/alpha/](http://releases.qt-project.org/qt5.0/alpha/)

Build instructions and Dependancies

[http://qt-project.org/wiki/Building_Qt_5_from_Git](http://qt-project.org/wiki/Building_Qt_5_from_Git)

[http://qt-project.org/wiki/Qt-5-Alph...g-instructions]("http://qt-project.org/wiki/Qt-5-Alpha-building-instructions")
I will show what I add as dependencies and keep it short as these are where it seems to give me problems
if you install the dependencies for QT5 as I do there is more chance you will get a working system

but the full build is massive ..... and 10 Gig is what I would advise to have free on any system if you are
going to attempt to build all of it .... ( it may be worth waiting for the finished product - as this is really time consuming too )

But I enjoy it ..... so round 2 ..... ;)

sudo apt-get install build-essential perl python git

sudo apt-get install ^libxcb.* libx11-xcb-dev libglu1-mesa-dev libxrender-dev

sudo apt-get install libedit-dev

sudo apt-get install flex bison gperf libicu-dev

sudo apt-get install libxcomposite-dev

sudo apt-get install libwayland-dev

Now going to re-run the 0.85 scrtpt Darxus made as this will go through the full build sequence again .....

 [http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/wayland-build-0.85.sh](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/buildscript/wayland-build-0.85.sh)

fingers crossed ..... ( if I get any more errors - I will post them )

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-08
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Well I give up, ran out of things to try, I get differant errors about missing files, seg fault 11, automake crash, I installed some extras to see what would happen, I guess thats why I get differant errors, I'll wait for some better news
):P

I have something unpleasant to say.  I had forgotten that signal 11 was the same as a segfault, but the way you said that reminded me, which reminded me that a signal 11 is often associated with hardware problems.

Some discussion of that phenominon here:  [http://www.bitwizard.nl/sig11/](http://www.bitwizard.nl/sig11/)
(first google hit on signal 11)

possibly memory ..... or hard drive ..... problems ..... could do some checks on your system
just to make sure ..... there is a smart monitor for hard drives 
( they are the one thing that do seem to wear out ..... but the [[COLOR=Blue]_**smart monitor**_[/COLOR]]("https://www.google.com/search?client=ubuntu&channel=fs&q=linux+hard+drive+smartmon&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8") tool - is good at reporting probs )

But

( if it compiles ok on the Arch system though then hardware is not the problem - but I think you had problems there too - were they segfault errors though )  

Worth looking on your other systems - see if you are getting a similar error .....

### Post by cecilpierce on 2012-04-08
> **Darxus said:**
> I have something unpleasant to say.  I had forgotten that signal 11 was the same as a segfault, but the way you said that reminded me, which reminded me that a signal 11 is often associated with hardware problems.

Some discussion of that phenominon here:  [http://www.bitwizard.nl/sig11/](http://www.bitwizard.nl/sig11/)
(first google hit on signal 11)

That was alot of info, I can only run 2 gigs on this machine, I have another one I can try just to see what happens, Thanks

Something that I just looked at again .... you had set swap space to only one Gig or there abouts ....

Think it should be better at [COLOR=Red]**4 Gig**[/COLOR]

I read this .... and it says the swap should be 2 x your internal Memory ...... so in your case on the machine you were 
previously using  - it may be as well to up this .... to 4 Gig ...... see if it helps .....

[http://www.centos.org/docs/5/html/5.1/Deployment_Guide/s1-swap-what-is.html](http://www.centos.org/docs/5/html/5.1/Deployment_Guide/s1-swap-what-is.html)

Was looking for reasons why the seg 11 error came up ......... and that is the only thing that has come to mind
so far ...... but it may be worth checking your logs too ....

I hope the new machine has plenty of memory too ...... 

( as you can see from my compile on a dual core machine with 4 Gig memory - it took 6 hours - or there abouts to complete )

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-09
> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
Finished at 7:24 am

...

Still seeing errors though ,,,,


```


[COLOR=Red][B]make[1]: *** [module-qtwebkit-install] Error 2
make[1]: Leaving directory `/home/keith/source/qt5'
make: *** [sub-qtwebkit-pri-install_subtargets] Error 2[/B][/COLOR]

```

Ouch.  What that's saying is, somewhere possibly way before that, there was an error.  I recommend running the script through nohup ("nohup /usr/bin/time ./wayland-build-0.85.sh") so it writes all of the output to nohup.out.  You can tail -f nohup.out to watch the output, and then you can grep -i the whole thing for error.  Fortunately, you should be able to just do "cd ~/install/qt5; make" and it'll start over where it left off, and just show you the error.  


> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
something that i just looked at again .... You had set swap space to only one gig or there abouts ....

Think it should be better at [color=red]**4 gig**[/color]

i read this .... And it says the swap should be 2 x your internal memory ...... 

That shouldn't cause build problems.  I suppose it could be possible with that much memory that you could run out, and it could die with an error.  But then you'd get a specific out of memory error.  But I bet you're not using nearly all of your 2gb just to compile.


> **cecilpierce said:**
> Too much strain for me, just came back from eye dr
I bet that's common among people likely to end up in this thread.  I make a point of looking out a window at something far away often.  My monitor at home is at least 4 feet from my eyes.  Similarly, I try to make sure my wrists get moved around often.

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-10
23dornot23d:  The "same file... error (ignored)" errors are not a problem, ignore them as the build says it is.  It's trying to install a file where it already is, realizes its already there, and that it's fine.

mkdir: cannot create directory `/usr/local/Trolltech': Permission denied

If you're not specifying an install prefix of /usr/local or /usr/local/Trolltech, that's a bug, introduced more recently than I've tried to build it, and you should tell #qt-labs on irc.freenode.net.

If you *did* specify an install prefix, don't do that, it doesn't work yet.  I'm guessing you didn't.

And I *really* wouldn't run that through sudo, I don't trust it nearly that much.  Although maybe you don't care about that OS install.


> **cecilpierce said:**
> I installed all the weston stuff in 12.04 and just dl the demos for Oneiric but havent tried it yet, it was deleted from precise for some reason ?
Let me know how it works in kubuntu.

The oneiric ver did not work, wrong lib's.

Yes, the weston package in ubuntu 12.04 works.  

Nothing was deleted from precise.  wayland-demos was renamed to weston before the 0.85 release.  However, the guy who packaged weston didn't include anything other than weston itself and weston-terminal, just because he hasn't had the opportunity yet (KiBi in #wayland).

---

### Post by cecilpierce on 2012-04-10
@Darxus

What does this mean: XDG_RUNTIME_DIR not set, falling back to .
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunProps
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunFront
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunOpen

Get that in the terminal when runnig weston, Thanks

---

### Post by Darxus on 2012-04-10
> **cecilpierce said:**
> @Darxus

What does this mean: XDG_RUNTIME_DIR not set, falling back to .
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunProps
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunFront
Internal error:   Could not resolve keysym SunOpen

Get that in the terminal when runnig weston, Thanks

Wayland uses the environment variable $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR to decide where it's going to put its socket file.  If you don't specify it, it uses ".", the current directory.  Which means you then need to make sure you run the clients from the same directory so they can find the socket.  

I'm guessing you're running the Ubuntu packages.  If you're running stuff installed to ~/install, there are a number of other environment variables you need set to point to the stuff in ~/install.

---

### Post by cecilpierce on 2012-04-10
> **Darxus said:**
> Wayland uses the environment variable $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR to decide where it's going to put its socket file.  If you don't specify it, it uses ".", the current directory.  Which means you then need to make sure you run the clients from the same directory so they can find the socket.  

I'm guessing you're running the Ubuntu packages.  If you're running stuff installed to ~/install, there are a number of other environment variables you need set to point to the stuff in ~/install.

Yes, ubuntu 12.04, never could get the other to compile, errors galore, thanks

If you ever happen to want to link against installed libraries
in a given directory, LIBDIR, you must either use libtool, and
specify the full pathname of the library, or use the `-LLIBDIR'
flag during linking and do at least one of the following:
   - add LIBDIR to the `[[COLOR=Blue]_**LD_LIBRARY_PATH**_[/COLOR]]("http://tldp.org/HOWTO/Program-Library-HOWTO/shared-libraries.html")' environment variable
     during execution
   - add LIBDIR to the `LD_RUN_PATH' environment variable
     during linking
   - use the `-Wl,-rpath -Wl,LIBDIR' linker flag
   - have your system administrator add LIBDIR to `/etc/ld.so.conf'

> 
LD_LIBRARY_PATH [COLOR=Red]**is handy for development and testing, but shouldn't be modified by an installation process for normal use by normal users**[/COLOR]; see ``Why LD_LIBRARY_PATH is Bad'' at [http://www.visi.com/~barr/ldpath.html]("http://www.visi.com/%7Ebarr/ldpath.html") for an explanation of why. But it's still useful for development or testing, and for working around problems that can't be worked around otherwise. If you don't want to set the LD_LIBRARY_PATH environment variable, on Linux you can even invoke the program loader directly and pass it arguments. For example, the following will use the given PATH instead of the content of the environment variable LD_LIBRARY_PATH, and run the given executable:

Re: Post Your Grub 2 Themes
A very simple Grub2 theme is part of my artwork proposal for Debian and can be found here:

[http://wiki.debian.org/DebianArt/The...Grub_2_-_Theme](http://wiki.debian.org/DebianArt/The...Grub_2_-_Theme)

It is supposed to look similar to the default GDM3 login screen. It is based on an Ubuntu theme
created by Jo Shields at [http://retro.apebox.org/grubthemes/](http://retro.apebox.org/grubthemes/)

The guide at [http://www.4shared.com/file/lFCl6wxL/grub_guidetar.html](http://www.4shared.com/file/lFCl6wxL/grub_guidetar.html)
was very helpful. Thanks a lot!

regards
Ulrich

software nowadays blows me away ..... 

I started on a Mono [[COLOR=Blue]_**Korg MS20**_[/COLOR]]("http://www.korg.co.uk/images/products/software_controllers/legacy/Legacy_ms20_01.png") and then a [[COLOR=Blue]_**Roland MT32 Synth**_[/COLOR]]("http://www.vintagesynth.com/roland/mt32.php") .... this used to be linked to my Atari 512 and was brilliant ... [[COLOR=Blue]_**512 k memory**_[/COLOR]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Atari_ST") on that and a [[COLOR=Blue]_**10 Meg hard drive**_[/COLOR]]("http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/History_of_hard_disk_drives") massive at the time ..... my friend was a IBM engineer and added it to the Atari
with a SCSI card - and what do we do with all the storage space nowadays

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-04-23
I was thinking about this today .....

Imagine if during the time you use your computer it records every module and every application ... kernel ... device managers etc ......

Now ..... heres the things ...... imagine that this program then dumped everything off of your computer that you never used ....... 

[COLOR=Blue]**To make a LiveCD or Dvd ..... from the only files and apps device drivers and kernel etc that you need ....**[/COLOR]

Wouldn't this be more efficient than what we try now ......

My problem is .....

keith@keith-Aspire-7730ZG:/var/log$ df
Filesystem     1K-blocks     Used Available Use% Mounted on
/dev/sda13     102296324 [COLOR=Red]**30,712,720**[/COLOR]  66460996  32% /
udev             1535688        4   1535684   1% /dev
tmpfs             618588     1008    617580   1% /run
none                5120        0      5120   0% /run/lock
none             1546460      460   1546000   1% /run/shm
keith@keith-Aspire-7730ZG:/var/log$ 

in a month my install goes from around 6 Gig  to 30 Gig  [COLOR=Red]**(30,712,720)**[/COLOR] .......... this does not
easily fit on a CD / Dvd ...... if it did I would share it with everyone ....

Now imagine this ..... a Database keeps track of the programs you use and need for that month ........

Anything else can be dumped .....

Leaving you with a simple way to create a small backup or distribution .....

I think too much ...... :lolflag:

Will try to cut this down .... but who knows if I will remove something important :confused:

But the way I choose to work now .... is to use what is available already .... 
but I use with the cautions mentioned ..... ( to be able to purge things may be needed ) but
at the moment I followed the instructions below .....  
only I use aptitude update and aptitude safe-upgrade as I tend to trust these commands more than apt-get
[http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-April/003009.html](http://lists.freedesktop.org/archives/wayland-devel/2012-April/003009.html)

### Post by Darxus on 2012-05-09
I don't think I mentioned my Ubuntu Precise GTK+ PPA here:  [https://launchpad.net/~darxus/+archive/wayland-gtk](https://launchpad.net/~darxus/+archive/wayland-gtk)
It contains the gtk packages rebuilt with the wayland backend enabled.  Hoping to get it included in Ubuntu Quantal.


I created a Qt5 binary tarball that includes qtwebkit.  It's just fancybrowser (qtwebkit) plus all the .so files.  It will only work on 64 bit Ubuntu Oneric, and I can't say I have high hopes of it working there, but you're welcome to try:  [http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/dl/qt5.binary.tar.xz](http://www.chaosreigns.com/wayland/dl/qt5.binary.tar.xz)

I suspect you need to extract that in /opt.  To run qtwebkit, with all the necessary environment varaibles set, run: /opt/qt5/qtwebkit-examples-and-demos/examples/webkit/fancybrowser/fancybrowser -platform wayland 

I'd be curious to hear if anybody gets it to work.  I continue to find building Qt5 to be incredibly unpleasant.  


> **23dornot23d said:**
> 
Now imagine this ..... a Database keeps track of the programs you use and need for that month ........


Popularity contest does this:  [http://popcon.ubuntu.com/](http://popcon.ubuntu.com/)

### Post by cbowman57 on 2012-05-26
Ok, just need a ```
sudo rm /opt/qt5/plugins/accessible/libqtaccessiblequick.so
``` then re-run it, looked ok from what I saw.  That virtual memory might clear itself after reboot, or add a swapfile or partition.

What's the "Rebecca Black"?

> 
Next, create a directory to chroot and mount existing partitions:
# mkdir /chroot
# d=/chroot
Mount actual partition:
# mount /dev/sda3 $d
Mount /proc and other file system inside $d, enter:
# mount -o bind /dev $d/dev
# mount -o bind /sys $d/sys
# mount -o bind /dev/shm $d/dev/shm
# mount -o bind /proc $d/proc
Chroot and access your data:
# chroot $d
# df
# ls
# vi /etc/somefile

Exit and reboot the system:
# exit
# umount $d/proc
# umount $d/sys
# umount $d/dev/shm
# umount $d/dev
# umount /dev/sda3


I have done this and can get into the system ....... just worried if I do a

### Post by cbowman57 on 2012-05-28
Or copy over the update-grub script from Ubuntu. :)

sudo grub-mkconfig -o /boot/grub/grub.cfg

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-06-03
If you have been loading weston or wayland then at some point it removes libdrm

and replaces it with libdrm-new

I found by putting libdrm back it all would work again ...... 

(  thing is if you have been compiling loads of other things - then it may get complex )

sudo pacman -Sy libdrm

( chicken egg scenario then - as weston needs libdrm-new - this is where I stopped working on it )

---

### Post by cbowman57 on 2012-06-03
You could download the tarballs, edit the PKGBUILD to echo darxus script build commands & run ```
makepkg -si --skipinteg 
```to ignore the md5.

(-csi if you want to clean out the source folders, etc)

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-06-05
The IRC does not keep a record - and I need to see what I have done and be able to replicate it ..... IRC is for young fresh minds - and quick fix solutions.

If what we say is here in the open right or wrong - people can look over it dismiss it or add a answer.

The time it takes to add a answer is little compared to the build time and lots of people hitting the same problem over and over.

One good answer to one easy problem for some and its team effort and we are away again .... rarely get stuck for too long .... at some point there will be a fully working build

> You could download the tarballs, edit the PKGBUILD to echo darxus  script build commands & run makepkg -si --skipinteg to ignore the  md5.

### Post by 23dornot23d on 2012-06-16
He looks to be one of the good guys that work on problem solving by giving examples and as much useful information as possible .....

No matter what a persons skill level this is a good blog post by him to read ......

[http://www.macieira.org/blog/2012/05/doesnt-work-doesnt-work/](http://www.macieira.org/blog/2012/05/doesnt-work-doesnt-work/)

Everyone that stays quiet and does not post anything is helping hide the fact that they come across problems and worse .... making out that none exist.

I see people post it doesn't work and used to ask relative questions to get more information from them .....

That wayland video shows just how far and fast things are progressing and gives me hope again ...... maybe in 6 months time we will have a proper working desktop.

As what was posted does not look too far away from a workable desktop - but obviously there will be lots of quirks to sort out before it could go public ......

Nice to see though and will now subscribe to [[COLOR=Blue]_***Thiago Macieira***_[/COLOR]]("https://www.google.com/search?client=ubuntu&channel=fs&q=Thiago+Macieira&ie=utf-8&oe=utf-8#hl=en&client=ubuntu&hs=v4s&channel=fs&sclient=psy-ab&q=Thiago+Macieira+2012&oq=Thiago+Macieira+2012&aq=f&aqi=&aql=&gs_l=serp.3...133953.139657.0.140107.9.5.2.2.2.0.1498.2837.0j2j1j6-1j1.5.0...0.0.H3xVpJD_vdg&pbx=1&bav=on.2,or.r_gc.r_pw.r_qf.,cf.osb&fp=fb4c575ddcccef5a&biw=1154&bih=515") as he seems to be doing
very well with programming and is going to the QT conference ...... maybe we will see
a full version of QT5 soon .....

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-06-21
> **cecilpierce said:**
> @nerdopolis

Does RBL get the updates that are in your 'svn' when I do apt-get update & upgrade ?

p.s. whats up or new Keith ?


I don't know if your version has it, but I added an update-rbos command which adds the changes from the SVN.

It also calls the build scripts to update those packages too. That might take a long time

It's not a PPA/repository so it won't get it's specific changes off of apt-get

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-07-28
I now have an updated Wayland ISO here:

[https://sourceforge.net/p/rebeccablackos/](https://sourceforge.net/p/rebeccablackos/)

It doesn't have qt, clutter, or SDL but probably within the few days, I should release a new ISO with QT, and SDL. I need to find an updated Clutter. It hasn't been changed since 0.85.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-07-29
I also developed a way to make it even easier to test Wayland by creating a script that automatically mounts and chroots the squashfs FS on my ISO. The ISO needs to be in your home directory, as it searches, and gives you a list of .isos to select that are in your home folder.

It installs dialog, aufs-tools, and squashfs-tools.

It also requires root access. But once you run it, it gives you a shell where you can run the command:
```
westoncaller
```
here is the link to the script
[http://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/test_RBOS_ISO.sh/download](http://sourceforge.net/projects/rebeccablackos/files/test_RBOS_ISO.sh/download)

### Post by lehjr on 2012-07-30
Just an FYI, I just tried the CD and it looks like it's using the 3.5 kernel. Problem is that it has a nasty bug in combination with certain hardware that the developers were aware of but still went ahead and finalized the kernel without applying the patch to fix it first #-o.

[https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1022351](https://bugs.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+source/linux/+bug/1022351)

I pulled the effected pci card and it boots and runs OK. There's still another issue where using "westoncaller" runs but as soon as I click on anything it drops back to a command prompt with error.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-07-30
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Im not sure what you mean but it says aufs-tools is already the newest version now.
When I try to run from a tty, says 'no drm device found', would that have anything to do with using nvidia-current driver?
I also get a 'Choose The Display server - Xorg or Wayland' in tty1 before anything and does not matter which I choose it runs into KDM ?

Sounds like the video card isn't supported by KMS. If you choose Wayland and your card isn't supported (or westton crashes) it falls back to KDM

LOL. I dunno, I'm still waiting to see official progress on what to do with XDG_RUNTIME_DIR. ```

### Post by lehjr on 2012-07-31
> **nerdopolis said:**
> hmm... could it be a driver issue? My box is only an intel...

On my Quantal installation I'm using the radeon driver package from the PPA shown here: [https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/desktop-q-system-compositor](https://blueprints.launchpad.net/ubuntu/+spec/desktop-q-system-compositor) 

which shows up as this in Synaptic:
**1:6.14.99~really6.14.5+git20120712.gitg8dc07e6-0~systemcompositor1**

On my Quantal installation, I use weston-launch (**export XDG_RUNTIME_DIR=/tmp && weston-launch**) and it is a bit more stable for me than what you have on the live cd, but it's no where near as interesting.

1. The first session option is just a Weston session. (with weston-desktop-shell) just the repeating background and the blue panel at the top with the launchers.
           (With xwayland KDE could work in Wayland, but weston window management is still limited, and plasma-desktop and the panels don't align properly yet)

2. Correct. It runs only Wayland in an xserver.

3. Correct.

EDIT: Ah. I see the confusion. Both the two options has the integrated X11 thing enabled, Just the first one Weston runs as a display server, and the second, weston is just a full screen x window (for video cards that don't have the KMS drivers)

### Post by lehjr on 2012-08-08

Well, here's what I know: 

Fedora has only a single Live CD with wayland, and that's from back in April and likely won't be updated until FC18.

There is a PPA with a working version, but it's an older version  and has no demos.

Supposedly all the newer builds don't even work without Intel based gfx because of a mesa bug, at least according to what I was told in #wayland a few days ago (you should have logs of this conversation since you were in the channel at the time). I don't even know why your build even works at all for me. The xorg-edgers PPA version certainly doesn't.

So yeah, your LiveCD is about the only way for anyone to test wayland, unless they want to build it themselves and replicate your work. nVidia users may not be so lucky, at least everything I've read is less than optimistic. But everything I have here is AMD/ATI based, so I have no way to test that.

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-08-10
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Can the change be added to the last version or have to DL the new one ?

Thanks

If you have it installed, you can run 
```
sudo update-rbos
```
and you'll see the change the next time you login

You'll have to hit CTRL-C when it hits the 15 second delay so it doesn't recompile source code. It doesn't need anything to compile. It's just a script that changed, and it will download for you.

### Post by lehjr on 2012-08-17
> **cecilpierce said:**

Normally, like this: [https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2/ISOBoot](https://help.ubuntu.com/community/Grub2/ISOBoot)

64 bit OS ISOs work fine, but 32 bit ones may not if you haven a NTFS partition. The problem is outlined in this thread (page 2 is where the problem is finally diagnosed): [http://forums.linuxmint.com/viewtopic.php?f=46&t=103062](http://forums.linuxmint.com/viewtopic.php?f=46&t=103062)

### Post by lehjr on 2012-08-18
> **nerdopolis said:**
> You made it to Phoronix article: [http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTE2MjY](http://www.phoronix.com/scan.php?page=news_item&px=MTE2MjY)

:lolflag:

The general tone of the resulting discussion is pretty much that what's needed is for nVidia to support nouveau rather than doing everything with binary blobs. 

I have to agree because the closed source drivers do hinder progress and will always lag behind current X-server/Wayland rather than being developed in parallel with it like the open source drivers. If the GPU manufactures would put the same amount of resources into helping with the opensource drivers as they do the closed source ones, we'd likely have better performance.

I should point out that I didn't always feel that way. I've been a user of ATI's "fglrx" driver for a long time, but they can't keep up with overall Linux changes, not just the kernel, but the overall combination of software that makes up the Desktop OS. Even looking at kernel updates alone, I think Ubuntu Quantal is getting a new kernel about every week now. That's not even considering other parts like the DE. The GPU driver teams probably wait until each stable release until they begin coding, which means they'll always be building tomorrow's drivers on yesterday's Linux (desktop OS combination).

Compare that to Windows, where Microsoft releases a new version of Windows every few years. While each release may have differences from the last that require new drivers, the release itself doesn't usually change to the point that the GPU driver team would have to change the driver with each update. And yet the GPU driver teams still release several updates for these drivers over the life span of each incarnation of Windows, often not really fixing everything until the final driver version, if at all.

I do suspect though that nVidia will hold off as long as they possibly can on Wayland support while claiming "lack of interest" from end users which will result in lack of interest from end users since they can't even be able to try Wayland, let alone use it without the required drivers. ATI/AMD would be wise to take advantage of this.

### Post by lehjr on 2012-08-23
> **cecilpierce said:**
> I tried to boot an iso and no luck either, let us know how you do.

Basically, **nerdopolis** needs to install **lupin-casper** before making ISO in order for the ISO itself to be have loop support. Also, if needed, this can be added to **/boot/grub/loopback.cfg** if it isn't automatically generated:```
menuentry "Try RebeccaBlackLinux without installing" {
	linux /casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper iso-scan/filename=${iso_path} quiet splash --
	initrd /casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Start RebeccaBlackLinux in safe graphics mode" {
	linux /casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper xforcevesa iso-scan/filename=${iso_path} quiet splash --
	initrd /casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Start RebeccaBlackLinux (compatibility mode)" {
	linux /casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper xforcevesa iso-scan/filename=${iso_path} ramdisk_size=1048576 root=/dev/ram rw noapic noapci nosplash irqpoll --
	initrd /casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Install RebeccaBlackLinux" {
	linux /casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper only-ubiquity iso-scan/filename=${iso_path} quiet splash --
	initrd /casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Test memory" {
	linux /install/memtest
}

```

Essentially, that's a GRUB2 cfg file. I don't believe it is required, but it allows the user to access the menu from the main GRUB2 installation with this added to **/etc/grub.d/40_custom**
```

menuentry "RebeccaBlackLinux" {
  iso_path="/home/leon/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
  export iso_path
  search --set --file $iso_path
  loopback loop $iso_path
  root=(loop)
  configfile /boot/grub/loopback.cfg
  loopback --delete loop
}
```
save and then run **sudo update-grub**
**Notes:**
[LIST]
[*]spaces in the ISO name may cause problems
[*]change the value for iso_path to match where you have your ISO.
[/LIST]

Again though, this would only work with **lupin-casper** installed.

---

### Post by lehjr on 2012-08-24
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Just tried the 'Unetbootin' trick, it didnt work for some reason, im lost.

This was added to the end of grub.cfg by unetbootin:

menuentry "UNetbootin" {
        set root=(hd0,1)
        linux  /boot/ubnkern file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper quiet splash --
        initrd /boot/ubninit
}
Yeah, that looks like it's missing some information. Maybe it gets some information from the loopback.cfg file, not sure. You'll probably want to get rid of the unetbootin entry. This is the full menu for the CD that you can add to 40_custom that should work for you. You don't have to add all of the entries, just the ones you want to use. Note that (hd0,1) means to look for the ISO in the first hard drive and the first partition. 

```

#!/bin/sh
exec tail -n +3 $0
# This file provides an easy way to add custom menu entries.  Simply type the
# menu entries you want to add after this comment.  Be careful not to change
# the 'exec tail' line above.

menuentry "Try RebeccaBlackLinux without installing" {
	set isofile="/home/cecil/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
	loopback loop (hd0,1)$isofile
	linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper initrd=/casper/initrd.gz iso-scan/filename=$isofile noeject noprompt quiet splash --	
	initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Start RebeccaBlackLinux in safe graphics mode" {
	set isofile="/home/cecil/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
	loopback loop (hd0,1)$isofile
	linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper xforcevesa initrd=/casper/initrd.gz iso-scan/filename=$isofile noeject noprompt quiet splash --
	initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Start RebeccaBlackLinux (compatibility mode)" {
	set isofile="/home/cecil/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
	loopback loop (hd0,1)$isofile
	linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper xforcevesa initrd=/casper/initrd.gz iso-scan/filename=$isofile ramdisk_size=1048576 root=/dev/ram rw noapic noapci nosplash noeject irqpoll --
	initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Install RebeccaBlackLinux" {
	set isofile="/home/cecil/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
	loopback loop (hd0,1)$isofile
	#loopback loop $iso_path
	linux (loop)/casper/vmlinuz file=/cdrom/preseed/custom.seed boot=casper only-ubiquity initrd=/casper/initrd.gz iso-scan/filename=$isofile noeject noprompt splash --
	initrd (loop)/casper/initrd.gz
}

menuentry "Test memory" {
	set isofile="/home/cecil/ISO/RebeccaBlackLinux.iso"
	loopback loop (hd0,1)$isofile
	linux (loop)/install/memtest
}

```

This should be good enough if you aren't working with a bunch of different CD's.

Reminds me of a post from awhile back where Linus Torvalds mentions compiling the Linux kernel in 19 seconds:

[https://plus.google.com/+LinusTorvalds/posts/6BxnSisp8fU](https://plus.google.com/+LinusTorvalds/posts/6BxnSisp8fU)


"...I must have ADHD, because I'm profiling the kernel build process where "make -j32" from a clean tree on my config takes 19 seconds, and I want to shave it down a bit more."


"...two cores with HT (four threads).

And enough memory to keep everything in RAM, together with ccache and an unhealthy amount of time spent on making sure pathname lookup is really really fast.

And I don't build modules I don't have. I do the occasional "build everything" just to make sure things don't break, but what I care about is what I can actually boot and test, so that's the thing I build all the time." &#65279;

Yes. That's for if you want to unmount the CD image. Use the arrow keys and hit enter. You probably would want to say "no" as in you don't want to leave the images mounted, and have the iso unmounted

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-09-06
> **cecilpierce said:**
> After I run script it just goes back to cmd prompt, maybe I have a bad DL ?


yes. it returned to the shell that you started the script with.

When you start the script with the CD image, it gives you a shell chrooted under the mounted FS with a user account of liveuser. when you are done you enter in exit, it brings the prompts, then returns you to your shell...

I probably should make a graphical version of the script....

### Post by nerdopolis on 2012-09-06
> **cecilpierce said:**
> Just checked synaptic, aufs is installed.

When I run the script now its asking for my password, it didnt do that before.

And this comes up:

The script has /usr/bin/sudo /bin/bash as the interpreter. Perhaps your sudo credentials expired, and that's why it's asking again.

As far as aufs, that IS strange... aufs.ko is provided by the kernel package, and aufs-tools is provided by aufs-tools.

your kernel doesn't have aufs.ko...  are you using a stock Ubuntu kernel?
try 
```
cd /lib
find grep | aufs
```

Wait, are you trying to use the test script within RBOS? I remember you said something about a 3.5 kernel... that's used in Quantal... I think I read something about Quantal dropping aufs, and it's actually using overlayfs... 

But you do NOT need to run the test script within RBOS... The test script is for people who don't want to use a VM, or reboot to test the Wayland userspace. The test_RBOS script uses chroot in the background.

If you are in RBOS, you just need to run westoncaller...
